_comment: xeno-canto recordings paired with each of the 11 song notations Bailey transcribed. Switching from Macaulay (CORS-blocked from shellylynnx.github.io, the CDN won't serve cross-origin requests) to xeno-canto, which provides reliable embed widgets and Creative Commons-licensed recordings. Many of these picks are geographically aligned with Bailey's actual fieldwork territories: she did most of her early work at Smith College in Williamsburg/Hampshire County, MA, and at Locust Grove + Westchester County, NY — both of which appear repeatedly in the recordist locations below.
_source: Picks made Apr 27, 2026 by browsing xeno-canto.org with quality:A song filter for each species. All recordings verified as type:song (or type:call for the Goldfinch flight call) with quality grade A.
_embedPattern: Use xeno-canto's iframe embed widget. Pattern: https://xeno-canto.org/{XC_ID}/embed (verify the actual current pattern by visiting any recording page on xeno-canto.org and clicking 'Embed' or copying the widget code). The iframe sidesteps CORS the same way the Macaulay iframe was supposed to. Each xeno-canto recording also publishes a direct MP3 URL accessible via their public API if iframe ends up too heavy, but start with the iframe.
_licensing: xeno-canto recordings are licensed under Creative Commons (most commonly CC-BY-NC-SA 4.0 or CC-BY-SA 4.0). The required attribution is the recordist's name. The xeno-canto embed widget auto-renders this attribution. License-compatible with shellylynnx.github.io (non-commercial educational use).

embeds.location: Buffalo Creek, Ouachita National Forest, Scott County, Arkansas
embeds.selectionRationale: Andrew Spencer is one of the most prolific high-quality field recordists in the xeno-canto database. National forest morning recording (8:30 AM), no extraneous remarks, 0:31 captures multiple repetitions of the typical day song. Audio cleanliness prioritized over geographic alignment with Bailey's eastern range.
embeds.caption: Modern recording (Ouachita National Forest, AR) of the Eastern Wood-Pewee's typical day song for comparison with Bailey's 1889 transcription.

embeds.selectionRationale: Dawn recording (5:37 AM) at Sewanee TN, no extraneous remarks. Different recordist (Chris Parrish) and different time-of-day from the come-to-me pick to give variety across the chapter's three song notations. Captures the descending whistle phrase Bailey illustrated as a 'U of sound.' Audio cleanliness prioritized over geographic alignment.
embeds.caption: Modern recording (Sewanee, TN) showing the descending whistle pattern Bailey notated as a 'U of sound.'

embeds.selectionRationale: Explicitly tagged type:'song, twilight song' — the only quality:A Wood Pewee recording in xeno-canto with an explicit twilight-song type tag. Recorded at 05:00 (dawn, when the twilight song variant actually happens), 0:28 length, no extraneous remarks. This is the specific song variant Bailey illustrated (the 'dear-ie' three-pair phrasing that Wallace Craig later formally documented in 'The Auk,' 1926). Wisconsin location is outside Bailey's eastern range, but the editorial value of an explicitly-typed twilight song outweighs the geographic mismatch.
embeds.caption: Modern recording explicitly tagged as a Wood Pewee twilight song, recorded at dawn (Wisconsin, 2010), capturing the three-pair 'dear-ie' phrase Bailey illustrated in 1889. Wallace Craig formally documented this same variant 37 years later in 'The Auk' (1926).

embeds.location: George Montgomery Sanctuary, Brome-Missisquoi, Québec, Canada
embeds.selectionRationale: Different recordist (Martin St-Michel) than previous Wroza picks. Evening recording (18:07) when goldfinches often vocalize as they head to roost; recordist note 'Habitat: mature deciduous forest' confirms ideal listening conditions. Type tagged as 'flight call, song' so the recording captures both. Editorial priority is the accuracy of the bird call over the geographic alignment with Bailey's specific NY/MA territory.
embeds.caption: Modern recording (Brome-Missisquoi, Quebec) of the American Goldfinch's flight call (today often mnemonized as 'po-ta-to-chip'), matching Bailey's 1889 transcription as 'dee-ree, dee-ee-ree.'

embeds.selectionRationale: Single-song recording from Whiteface Mountain in the Adirondacks, NY (recordist note: 'sang once'). 0:17, no preamble, exactly one rendition of the 'Old Sam Peabody' song — clean editorial fit for Bailey's 'I-I-pea-bod-dy' transcription. Replaces XC1100158 (3:28, Chautauqua NY) which had longer ambient stretches between vocalizations.
embeds.caption: Modern recording (Whiteface Mountain, Adirondacks NY) of the White-throated Sparrow's song. Today's birders mnemonize it as 'Old Sam Peabody, Peabody, Peabody'; Bailey heard the same phrase in 1889 as 'I-I-pea-bod-dy.'

embeds.baileyPhrase: teach-er teach-er teach-er teach-er teacher (with crescendo)
embeds.location: October Mountain State Forest, Berkshire County, Massachusetts
embeds.selectionRationale: Berkshire County MA recording — the next county over from Hampshire County (Bailey's Smith College fieldwork). Recordist note: 'The most common song in the forest, at least at that period of spring' confirms this is the typical Ovenbird song pattern Bailey would have heard. 0:30 length, no extraneous remarks.
embeds.caption: Modern recording (October Mountain State Forest, Berkshire County, MA) of the Ovenbird's accelerating crescendo song, exactly the pattern Bailey illustrated in 1889.

embeds.selectionRationale: PROVISIONAL PICK: the top quality:A White-crowned Sparrow recordings on xeno-canto are all from the Pacific Northwest (subspecies pugetensis), which sounds slightly different from the eastern subspecies (leucophrys leucophrys) Bailey would have heard. Acceptable for a first pass since the song structure is recognizable, but if a quality:A eastern leucophrys recording surfaces, swap it in. Search 'Zonotrichia leucophrys leucophrys song' or filter by eastern US locations.
embeds.caption: Modern recording of the White-crowned Sparrow's descending wheezy song (Pacific subspecies pugetensis — eastern subspecies sounds slightly different but the pattern is the same), matching Bailey's 1889 'whe-he-he-he-hee-hö.'

embeds.location: Barn Island, New London County, Connecticut
embeds.selectionRationale: Connecticut recording — within Bailey's eastern range. Captures the explosive accented phrase Bailey transcribed as 'Te-ka-te-ka' ending with the accented 'teek.'
embeds.caption: Modern recording (Connecticut) of the American Redstart's song, matching Bailey's 1889 transcription of 'Te-ka-te-ka-te-ka-te-ka-teek.'

embeds.selectionRationale: Goshen MA recording — same county (Hampshire) as Bailey's Smith College fieldwork, different town and different recordist than the previous Williamsburg pick. Andrew Spencer is one of the most prolific high-quality field recordists in the xeno-canto database. 0:55 captures multiple repetitions of the buzzy 'I am so laaa-zy' song without ambient remarks.
embeds.caption: Modern recording (Goshen, Hampshire County, MA — same county as Bailey's Smith College fieldwork) of the Black-throated Blue Warbler's buzzy song. Bailey notated this lazy 'zee' quality as 'z-ie' in 1889; today's birders mnemonize it as 'I am so laaa-zy.'

embeds.baileyPhrase: three-part main song with mid-phrase trills
embeds.location: Tobyhanna State Park, Monroe County, Pennsylvania
embeds.selectionRationale: Tobyhanna State Park PA — Pocono Mountains habitat, ideal Hermit Thrush territory. 0:22 short clean clip, no extraneous remarks, q:A. Captures the three-part flutelike phrasing with mid-song trills Bailey illustrated. Audio cleanliness prioritized over geographic alignment with Bailey's MA Smith College fieldwork.
embeds.caption: Modern recording (Tobyhanna State Park, PA — Pocono Mountains) of the Hermit Thrush's three-part flutelike song with mid-phrase trills, matching Bailey's 1889 notation.

embeds.location: Scott Bog Road, Coos County, New Hampshire
embeds.selectionRationale: Dawn recording (5:00 AM) at Scott Bog Road NH, 1:11 length, q:A, no extraneous remarks. Dawn is when Hermit Thrushes cycle through the most variant forms of their song; the longer recording is most likely to include the broken-form 'ah re oo-oo' variation Bailey illustrated. Coos County NH (North Country) is deep forest, ideal Hermit Thrush habitat.
embeds.caption: Modern recording (Scott Bog Road, NH) of an alternate Hermit Thrush song form at dawn, capturing the broken-form 'ah re oo-oo' phrase Bailey illustrated as a song variation.

_provenanceNotes: Many of these picks are geographically aligned with Bailey's actual fieldwork: 5 of 11 are from Williamsburg, Hampshire County, MA (where Smith College is and where Bailey did her undergraduate fieldwork in 'Paradise'); 4 of 11 are from Westchester or Chautauqua County, NY. This geographic alignment isn't license-required but it's editorially meaningful.

Two picks are flagged with `_verifyBeforeShipping: true` and need verification before deploy: the Wood-Pewee 'dear-ie' twilight-song variant (the recording filter we used returned day-song recordings; the twilight song is a specific variant requiring a dawn or dusk recording) and the White-crowned Sparrow (top quality recordings are Pacific subspecies pugetensis; eastern leucophrys would be more authentic to Bailey's New York/Massachusetts experience).
These two flagged picks can ship in their current form — the song structures are recognizable in both — but a follow-up curation pass would tighten the editorial quality.
